title: 1.1: Reports that libharfbuzz is loaded into the main Kitty process for text shaping (e.g., via ldd output showing libharfbuzz.so)
1.2: Reports that libfreetype is loaded into the main Kitty process for glyph rendering (e.g., via ldd output showing libfreetype.so)
1.3: Reports that fast_data_types.so is a core extension module in the main Kitty process
1.4: Reports thread activity differences between idle and stress states (e.g., main thread CPU usage increases significantly under load)
1.5: Describes a portability-versus-performance tradeoff observed at runtime (e.g., GLFW platform abstraction or software vs hardware rendering)
1.6: Reports that Kitty's control interface remains accessible during rendering pressure (e.g., via kitten @ ls command)
1.7: Reports that kitten runs as a separate process with its own PID, not loaded into the main Kitty process
1.8: Reports that the kitten executable is built with Go (e.g., by showing Go-specific ELF sections like .gosymtab or .gopclntab)
1.9: Reports a stack-level or symbol-level snapshot captured during stress (e.g., via gdb backtrace, nm symbols, or similar inspection)
1.10: Reports that C handles font shaping and rasterization (e.g., via HarfBuzz hb_shape or FreeType FT_* calls)
1.11: Reports that Python handles configuration, orchestration, or remote control in Kitty
1.12: Reports that Go is used for kitten utilities that run as separate processes
2.1: Claims that Python directly calls OpenGL rendering functions
2.2: Claims analysis conclusions without providing supporting runtime evidence
2.3: Claims that kitten communicates with Kitty through shared memory
